claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

expected_eligibility.name: Blake

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know their age, and if you have multiple children, please tell me about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wanted to check if I'm eligible.

I have two children I'd like to ask about. Alex is 11 years old, and Blake is 15. So both are under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Good news - since both Alex (11) and Blake (15) are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Now let me check the next requirement for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about work:**

Are both Alex and Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is typically straightforward for children of their ages, but I need to confirm this for each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're just in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they both meet this requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about benefits:**

Are both Alex and Blake **NOT receiving** any of the following benefits in their own right?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

In other words, are neither of the children claiming these benefits themselves?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's another requirement met for both children. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about apprenticeships:**

Are Alex and Blake currently enrolled in any apprenticeship programmes?

Most apprenticeships would disqualify a child from Child Benefit, with one exception being Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales. Since you mentioned they're both in school, I expect the answer is no - but I just need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not doing any apprenticeships. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming. Since both Alex and Blake are in regular school and not in any apprenticeships, they continue to meet all the child-related requirements. Now let's check the requirements for you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now let's check your residency status.**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you need to meet UK residency requirements. You can qualify if **any** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You are a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these situations apply to you? For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the relevant one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, actually I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, let me ask a follow-up question:

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?**

Crown servants include people who work for the UK government overseas, such as:
- Diplomatic staff
- Armed forces personnel stationed abroad
- Civil servants posted overseas

Or are you living in certain other qualifying countries in connection with Crown service?

If not, could you tell me a bit more about your situation? For example:
- Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?
- What is your connection to the UK?

This will help me determine if you might qualify through any of the residency pathways.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I don't have settled or pre-settled status either. I just live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, I need to deliver some disappointing news.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 11 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet residency requirements |
| **Blake** | 15 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet residency requirements |

### Why not eligible?

Both Alex and Blake fully meet all the child-related requirements for Child Benefit (they're under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in apprenticeships). However, **you must normally live in the UK** (or meet special circumstances) to claim Child Benefit.

### What you can do:

1. **If your circumstances change** - If you return to live in the UK, you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit at that point.

2. **If someone else in the UK cares for the children** - Another person who lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex and Blake may be able to claim Child Benefit instead.

3. **Check for other support** - Depending on where you live, there may be equivalent family benefits available in that country.

I'm sorry I couldn't bring better news. If your situation changes or you have any other questions, please don't hesitate to ask.

reasonings: Alex (age 11) meets all child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements as they currently live abroad and do not qualify through any of the special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, or Crown servant status).

Blake (age 15) meets all child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements as they currently live abroad and do not qualify through any of the special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, or Crown servant status).
arguments.overall_reasoning: While both children (Alex, 11, and Blake, 15) fully meet all the child-related eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, the claimant does not satisfy the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, nor are they a Crown servant. UK residency (or qualifying through one of the special circumstances) is a mandatory requirement for claiming Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements as they currently live abroad and do not qualify through any of the special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, or Crown servant status).
